notation.T: V → W: Notation for a linear transformation from V to W
notation.T(x): The output vector in W for an input vector x in V
notation.R²: The specific vector space of 2D real coordinate vectors
notation.k, θ, s: Scalar parameters for specific transformations (scale factor, angle, shear factor)
notation.R_θ: Rotation transformation matrix for angle θ
notation.i-hat, j-hat: The standard basis vectors for R², (1,0) and (0,1)
notation.a, b: The images of the basis vectors under T, i.e., a = T(i-hat), b = T(j-hat)
notation.A: The matrix representing the linear transformation T
notation.x₁, x₂: The coordinates (scalar components) of a vector x with respect to the standard basis
notation.S: A second linear transformation (often the first in a composition)
notation.T ∘ S: The composition of linear transformations, applying S first, then T
notation.U: A vector space (often the domain of the first transformation in a composition)
notation.B: The matrix representing the linear transformation S
notation.C: The matrix representing the composed transformation T∘S, equal to the matrix product A B
notation.Im(T), T(V): The image (range) of T, the set of all output vectors in W
notation.Ker(T), N(T): The kernel (null space) of T, the set of all input vectors in V that map to the zero vector in W
notation.nullity: The dimension of the kernel of T
notation.rank: The dimension of the image of T
notation.T^{-1}: The inverse linear transformation of T, satisfying T^{-1}∘T = I_V and T∘T^{-1} = I_W
notation.I_V, I_W: The identity transformation on vector space V and W, respectively
notation.det(A): The determinant of matrix A. A is invertible if and only if det(A) ≠ 0
notation.A^{-1}: The inverse matrix of A, satisfying A A^{-1} = A^{-1} A = I

objects.domain_plane: The coordinate grid representing the input vector space V
objects.codomain_plane: The coordinate grid representing the output vector space W
objects.general_mapping_label: The central 'T: V → W' text label
objects.property_equations: The MathTex group containing the additivity and homogeneity equations, often used as a reference
objects.shape_S: A reference square/object in the domain V to visualize the transformation
objects.transformed_shape_T_S: The image of shape S under T in the codomain W
objects.example_icons: Small visual reminders of scaling, rotation, and shear transformations
objects.basis_vectors_i_j: The standard basis vectors i-hat and j-hat in the domain plane V
objects.image_vectors_a_b: The images T(i-hat) and T(j-hat) in the codomain plane W
objects.matrix_A: The 2x2 matrix representing T, with columns a and b
objects.plane_U: A third coordinate plane representing the initial vector space U in a composition
objects.matrix_B: The 2x2 matrix representing the linear transformation S
objects.matrix_C: The 2x2 matrix representing the composed transformation T∘S = AB
objects.image_region: The visual representation (e.g., a line or plane) of the image subspace in the codomain_plane W
objects.kernel_line: The visual representation (e.g., a line) of the kernel subspace in the domain_plane V
objects.inverse_label_Tinv: The label 'T^{-1}: W → V' for the inverse transformation
objects.determinant_display: The visual display for calculating and showing det(A)
objects.summary_header: The persistent header containing 'T: V → W' and the linearity property equations.
objects.icon_row_scaling: A small animation icon showing a square scaling up.
objects.icon_row_rotation: A small animation icon showing a square rotating.
objects.icon_row_shear: A small animation icon showing a square shearing into a parallelogram.
objects.summary_representation_row: A final row of icons summarizing matrix, composition, and subspaces.

story_so_far: A linear transformation T is a function that maps vectors from one vector space V to another vector space W.
A linear transformation T: V → W is defined by two properties: Additivity T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v) and Homogeneity T(c u)=c T(u).
Linear transformations in 2D (like scaling, rotation, shear) map the entire space in a structured way: straight lines remain straight, parallel lines remain parallel, and the origin is fixed.
Any linear transformation T: Rⁿ → Rᵐ can be represented by an m×n matrix A, whose columns are the images of the standard basis vectors under T.
The composition of two linear transformations T∘S is also linear, and its matrix representation is the product of their individual matrices: C = A B, where A is for T and B is for S.

The kernel (null space) of a linear transformation T is the subspace of the domain V consisting of all vectors that map to the zero vector in W. The image (range) is the subspace of the codomain W consisting of all vectors that are outputs of T.

A linear transformation T is invertible if and only if it is one-to-one (kernel = {0}) and onto (image = all of W). This corresponds to its matrix representation A being a square matrix with a non-zero determinant, in which case the inverse transformation T⁻¹ exists and is represented by the inverse matrix A⁻¹.

The theory of linear transformations is built on the pillars of linearity (additivity/homogeneity), matrix representation, composition as matrix multiplication, the fundamental subspaces (kernel and image), and the invertibility condition (det ≠ 0).
constraints: The transformation is denoted by T (or similar). Input space is V, output space is W.
Input vectors are styled in BLUE_C, mapping arrows in PURE_GREEN.
The properties T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v) and T(c u)=c T(u) are the defining criteria for linearity.
When visualizing a specific transformation, the background grid of the codomain plane may be warped to visually represent the mapping of the entire space.
The matrix representing a linear transformation T is constructed by taking the images of the domain's standard basis vectors as its columns.
Composition of linear transformations corresponds to matrix multiplication in the same order: (T∘S) ↔ A B, meaning B (for S) is applied first.
The kernel is always a subspace of the domain V. The image is always a subspace of the codomain W.
For a linear transformation T: V→W, the zero vector in V is always in the kernel, and the zero vector in W is always in the image.
An invertible linear transformation T: V→W implies V and W have the same dimension. Its matrix A is square and has det(A) ≠ 0.
The inverse transformation T⁻¹, when it exists, is also linear and satisfies T⁻¹∘T = I_V and T∘T⁻¹ = I_W.
